The Beatles released 'Love Me Do' on 5 October 1962 — their debut single.
Mozart wrote his first symphony at age 8.
'Bohemian Rhapsody' by Queen took three weeks to record and cost £25,000 in 1975.
The guitar was invented in Spain in the 15th century.
Elvis Presley never performed outside North America after his early career.
Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' remains the best-selling album of all time.
The piano has 88 keys — 52 white and 36 black.
Bob Dylan won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016.
The world's oldest known musical instrument is a flute made from a vulture wing bone, around 40,000 years old.
Beethoven was almost completely deaf when he composed his Ninth Symphony.
'Happy Birthday to You' was written in 1893 and is one of the most recognised songs in the English language.
Jimi Hendrix could not read music.
The word 'music' comes from the Greek word 'mousike' — art of the Muses.
David Bowie created at least 15 different stage personas throughout his career.
The violin has four strings tuned in perfect fifths: G, D, A, E.
ABBA turned down one billion dollars to reunite in 2000.
Frank Sinatra was the first teen idol — girls fainted at his concerts in the 1940s.
'Imagine' by John Lennon was recorded in his home studio at Tittenhurst Park in just one day.
The first music video ever broadcast on MTV was 'Video Killed the Radio Star' by The Buggles in 1981.
Stevie Wonder was born six weeks premature, and his blindness was caused by too much oxygen in his incubator.
The fax machine was invented the same year as the telephone — 1876.
Tchaikovsky composed the 1812 Overture in just six weeks.
The word 'jazz' is first documented in print in 1912.
Led Zeppelin's 'Stairway to Heaven' has never been released as a single.
Pavarotti held a high C for 1 minute 15 seconds in one performance — a world record.
Freddie Mercury wrote 'Crazy Little Thing Called Love' in the bath in ten minutes.
The saxophone is named after its inventor, Adolphe Sax, who patented it in 1846.
Paul McCartney's 'Yesterday' is the most covered song in history, with over 2,200 recorded versions.
The human ear can detect sounds from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz — a range of about 10 octaves.
Debussy never allowed his Clair de Lune to be played at a normal concert pace — he felt it was always rushed.
The world's largest concert audience was 3.5 million people at Rod Stewart's free New Year's concert in Rio de Janeiro in 1994.
The shortest number one single in UK chart history is 'Ob-La-Di, Ob-La-Da' — Marmalade, 1968.
Chopin gave so few public recitals that his total lifetime concert appearances probably numbered under 30.
'Smells Like Teen Spirit' by Nirvana was inspired by a piece of graffiti on Kurt Cobain's bedroom wall.
The first album ever released on CD was ABBA's 'The Visitors' in 1981.
Music activates the cerebellum — the part of the brain responsible for movement, which is why we instinctively move to a beat.
The best-selling classical album ever is Andrea Bocelli's 'Romanza' (1996).
James Brown is sampled more than any other musician in hip-hop.
The ukulele originated in Hawaii but is derived from a Portuguese instrument called the machete.
Johnny Cash played his last concert at the Carter Family Fold in Hiltons, Virginia, just three months before he died.
The record for most number-one albums in the UK belongs to Elvis Presley with 13.
Aretha Franklin's voice spans almost four octaves.
The entire score of Handel's Messiah was composed in just 24 days.
'Gangnam Style' by PSY was the first YouTube video to reach one billion views in 2012.
Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' (1959) is the best-selling jazz album of all time.
The didgeridoo is one of the world's oldest wind instruments, used by Aboriginal Australians for at least 1,500 years.
Kate Bush was the first woman to achieve a UK number one with a self-written debut single — 'Wuthering Heights' in 1978.
The longest officially released song is 'The Rise and Fall of Bossanova' by PC III at 13 hours, 23 minutes.
Drummers burn around 400–600 calories per hour — comparable to an aerobics class.
Radiohead allowed fans to pay whatever they wanted for 'In Rainbows' in 2007 — the average payment was $6.
The theremin is the only musical instrument you play without touching it.
'White Christmas' by Bing Crosby is the best-selling physical single of all time with 50 million copies sold.
The term 'album' comes from the Latin for 'white tablet' — referring to the white tablets Romans used for public notices.
Whitney Houston's 'I Will Always Love You' was originally written and recorded by Dolly Parton in 1973.
The first gramophone record was made by Emile Berliner in 1887.
Yo-Yo Ma has played in every US state and on every continent including Antarctica.
The bass guitar was invented by Leo Fender in 1951.
KISS performed in full makeup and costumes for the first 9 years of their career before revealing their faces.
'Despacito' became the most-streamed song of all time in 2017, reaching 4.6 billion streams.
The Grateful Dead played over 2,300 concerts between 1965 and 1995.
Ravel's Bolero consists of a single melodic theme repeated 18 times over 15 minutes.
The longest concert ever performed was by Lluis Coloma, who played piano for 5 days straight.
Taylor Swift re-recorded her first six albums to reclaim ownership of her music.
Lady Gaga wrote 'Just Dance' in 10 minutes.
The word 'symphony' comes from the Greek 'symphonia', meaning agreement of sound.
'Don't Stop Believin' by Journey became the best-selling digital catalogue song in history.
Music has been used as medicine — the ancient Greeks used it to cure mental disorders.
Elton John has released over 30 studio albums and his songs have topped the charts in six consecutive decades.
The original Studio 54 disco ball weighed 365 kilograms.
The Star-Spangled Banner was originally written as a poem in 1814 — it didn't become the US national anthem until 1931.
Prince recorded over 500 unreleased songs that are kept in a vault at Paisley Park.
The triangle — the simplest orchestral instrument — requires as much skill as any other to play well.
'Hallelujah' by Leonard Cohen has been covered over 300 times by different artists.
Nicki Minaj is the female solo artist with the most Billboard Hot 100 entries.
The organ is one of the oldest keyboard instruments, dating back to ancient Greece.
'Yesterday' by The Beatles was originally titled 'Scrambled Eggs'.
Adele named her albums after her age when she wrote them — 19, 21, 25, 30.
The violin can produce over 90 distinct sounds through different bowing techniques.
Eminem can rap at approximately 10–11 syllables per second.
The first official music chart appeared in the US in 1913.
Chopin wrote almost exclusively for the piano — and barely ever heard his own music performed by others.
Bruce Springsteen once performed a 4-hour concert in Helsinki, Finland.
The first song to be streamed over the internet was 'Midnight in Chicago' by Severe Tire Damage in 1993.
Dolly Parton wrote 'I Will Always Love You' and 'Jolene' on the same day.
Vivaldi wrote over 500 concertos — nearly half of them for violin.
The most expensive musical instrument ever sold is a Stradivarius violin — 'The Hammer' — sold for $3.54 million.
RuPaul has been making music professionally since the late 1980s.
The harp is one of the oldest instruments known — harps appear in ancient Egyptian art from 3000 BC.
Bono has been a full-time rock star since age 16 and has never held another job.
The musical note A above middle C vibrates at exactly 440 Hz — a standard set in 1939.
David Bowie and Queen recorded 'Under Pressure' in one evening — a chance meeting at the recording studio.
The shortest piece of classical music ever performed professionally lasts 0.03 seconds.
Cher is the only artist to have had a number-one single in six consecutive decades.
The glockenspiel was first used in an orchestral piece by Handel in his opera 'Saul' in 1739.
'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star' is based on a French melody composed in the 18th century.
The oboe is considered the hardest woodwind instrument to play.
Bob Marley was buried with his guitar, a football and a bud of marijuana.
'What's Going On' by Marvin Gaye was originally rejected by Motown Records before becoming a classic.
A standard CD can hold up to 74 minutes of audio — chosen because that's the length of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony.
Eric Clapton is the only person inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame three times.
George Harrison wrote 'While My Guitar Gently Weeps' after randomly opening a book — the first words he read became the lyric.
The bass guitar was designed so that musicians could hear the bass part clearly in loud environments.
'Waterloo' by ABBA, which won Eurovision in 1974, was almost not submitted because the group wasn't sure it was good enough.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' stayed on the US Billboard chart for 31 years.
John Lennon was left-handed but played a right-handed guitar flipped upside down.
Metallica's 'Master of Puppets' is considered one of the greatest heavy metal songs ever written — and is taught in music schools.
Beethoven reportedly dipped his head in cold water before composing to stimulate his brain.
'Lose Yourself' by Eminem was written during the filming of '8 Mile' on a paper bag.
Diana Ross and The Supremes had 12 number-one singles in the US — more than any other act in the 1960s.
The electric guitar was invented in 1931 by George Beauchamp.
Celine Dion has sold over 200 million albums worldwide.
Music can reduce anxiety by up to 65%, according to University of Nevada research.
Puccini's opera 'Turandot' was left unfinished at his death in 1924 — it was completed by Franco Alfano.
The world's largest commercially available speaker is manufactured by Danley Sound Labs and can produce 110dB.
Lorde wrote and recorded 'Royals' in a single afternoon at age 16.
'Take On Me' by A-ha took three attempts and two years to become a hit.
The sound you hear when you put a shell to your ear is actually ambient noise from around you, amplified.
The record for the most Grammy Awards won by an individual is held by Beyoncé with 32 Grammys.
'Superstition' by Stevie Wonder was written for Jeff Beck, who gave it back because he didn't want to compete with Stevie's own recording.
Piano keys were originally made from elephant ivory — the transition to plastic began in the 1970s.
Mick Jagger and Keith Richards met on a train in 1961 when Jagger was carrying a stack of Chuck Berry and Muddy Waters albums.
'Africa' by Toto was covered by Weezer in 2018 because a 14-year-old fan campaigned on Twitter for two years.
The shortest piece in classical repertoire is John Cage's '4:33' — four minutes and 33 seconds of silence.
Kanye West is the most Grammy-nominated hip-hop artist of all time with 75 nominations.
The accordion was invented in Vienna in 1829.
'Born to Run' by Bruce Springsteen took six months to record.
Phil Collins was the first artist to sell out Wembley Stadium twice in a single day.
The oldest surviving musical notation is a Sumerian hymn from approximately 1400 BC.
The Spice Girls sold over 85 million records worldwide.
'Nessun Dorma' from Puccini's Turandot became one of the most recognised arias in the world after Pavarotti sang it at the 1990 World Cup.
The word 'chord' comes from 'accord', meaning agreement.
The best-selling Irish single of all time is 'Riverdance' by Bill Whelan.
'Rapper's Delight' by the Sugarhill Gang (1979) was the first rap song to enter the Billboard Top 40.
The Beatles' Abbey Road was the first album mastered digitally — in 1979, for its re-release.
Chuck Berry's 'Johnny B. Goode' was included on the Voyager Golden Record sent into space in 1977.
Katy Perry has had more number-one singles than any other female artist on the US Hot 100.
The first portable music player was the Sony Walkman, released in 1979.
Country music legend Hank Williams Sr. died on New Year's Day 1953 aged just 29.
In 1991, Nirvana's 'Nevermind' knocked Michael Jackson's 'Dangerous' off the top of the Billboard chart.
Ozzy Osbourne bit the head off a live dove during a meeting with a record label executive in 1981.
'We Are the World' was recorded by 45 artists in a single all-night session on 28 January 1985.
The most expensive music video ever made was Michael and Janet Jackson's 'Scream' (1995) at $7 million.
The world's most recorded composer is anonymous — the 'Greensleeves' melody has been recorded thousands of times.
Hip-hop became the most consumed music genre in the US in 2017, overtaking rock for the first time.
Paul Simon wrote 'The Sound of Silence' when he was just 21.
Louis Armstrong was buried with a trumpet mouthpiece.
The melody of 'We Wish You a Merry Christmas' dates back to 16th century England.
Billy Joel played piano in a bar for a year under a pseudonym because he couldn't afford to break his recording contract.
The harmonica is the world's best-selling music instrument.
The backing track of 'Under Pressure' was famously sampled without permission in Vanilla Ice's 'Ice Ice Baby'.
Nina Simone was rejected from the Curtis Institute of Music in 1950 — she always believed it was because of her race.
The word 'disco' comes from the French 'discothèque', a library of phonograph records.
Handel's Messiah was composed in 24 days — so fast that Handel said he felt God had visited him.
Kurt Cobain was left-handed but learned guitar on a right-handed instrument, which he then restrung.
The first iPod held 1,000 songs. The first iPhone held none — Jobs removed the iPod functionality at the last minute.
Tchaikovsky is said to have conducted while holding his chin with one hand, afraid his head would fall off.
Daft Punk wore their helmets so consistently that many people don't know what they look like.
The best-selling album of the 21st century so far is Adele's '21', with over 31 million copies sold.
'Yesterday' by The Beatles has been played on the radio over 7 million times.
Bob Dylan has been awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the Nobel Prize for Literature, and an Oscar.
The electric bass guitar was designed to replace the double bass, which was difficult to transport and amplify.
'We Will Rock You' by Queen was written specifically to give the audience something to do — stomp and clap.
Elton John has donated over $450 million to charity over his career.
The world's first music streaming service was Launch Media, founded in 1994.
Neil Young has sued himself — successfully — to regain rights to his music by claiming his own performance was substandard.
Whitney Houston's vocal range spanned three octaves — from a low C3 to a high G5.
'Stairway to Heaven' was not released as a single until 2012 — 41 years after it was recorded.
Shostakovich used musical notes to encode his own initials in his compositions: D-S-C-H.
The first recorded use of the phrase 'rock and roll' to describe music was in 1951.
Sir George Martin — the 'Fifth Beatle' — was responsible for orchestral arrangements on many Beatles songs.
The song 'Happy Birthday' was once copyrighted and companies paid royalties to use it until 2016.
'Billie Jean' by Michael Jackson was the first video by a Black artist to be played on MTV.
There are approximately 1.4 billion guitar players in the world.
Ringo Starr is considered by many drummers to be one of the most melodic drummers in rock history.
The Fender Stratocaster was designed by Leo Fender, who could not actually play the guitar.
Music appears in every known culture on Earth — there is no human civilisation without it.
Maria Callas could sing both soprano and mezzo-soprano roles — an extraordinary vocal range.
The recorder (the instrument children learn in school) was once considered a noble instrument played by kings.
'What a Wonderful World' by Louis Armstrong was rejected by US radio when it was released in 1967 — it only became a hit after being used in Good Morning, Vietnam in 1987.
The theremin was invented by Leon Theremin in 1920 and is still played in the same way today.
The longest stadium tour ever was U2's 360° Tour (2009–2011), which grossed $736 million.
'Sweet Home Alabama' by Lynyrd Skynyrd was written as a response to Neil Young's 'Southern Man'.
Freddie Mercury's vocal range was approximately F2 to F6 — nearly four octaves.
The national anthem of Spain has no lyrics — it is one of only four national anthems that are purely instrumental.
'Eye of the Tiger' by Survivor was written specifically for Rocky III after Sylvester Stallone couldn't get the rights to use 'Another One Bites the Dust'.
Tchaikovsky's 1812 Overture is one of the few classical compositions that calls for actual artillery cannon fire.
The Bee Gees wrote 'Islands in the Stream' for Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton in 45 minutes.
The original title for 'Every Breath You Take' by The Police was 'I'll Be Watching You'.
The oldest surviving piano was built by Bartolomeo Cristofori in 1720 and is on display in Rome.
Aretha Franklin was the first woman inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1987.
The song 'Jolene' is based on a real person — a red-headed bank teller who flirted with Dolly Parton's husband.
The bagpipes are not originally from Scotland — they originated in the Middle East over 3,000 years ago.
Gorillaz is the world's most successful virtual band.
Adele turned down the opportunity to write the Bond theme 'Skyfall' twice before agreeing to do it.
There are 24 major and minor keys in Western music.
Radiohead spent two years recording 'OK Computer' — widely regarded as one of the greatest albums ever made.
Glenn Gould, the famous Canadian pianist, hummed aloud while performing — it can be heard on his recordings.
The trumpet is one of the oldest instruments — trumpets have been found in Tutankhamun's tomb from 1323 BC.
'Born This Way' by Lady Gaga sold one million copies in five days — the fastest-selling single in iTunes history at the time.
Pharrell Williams's 'Happy' was one of the first songs released as a 24-hour music video.
George Michael was the first Western pop star to play in the Soviet Union, in 1988.
The world's largest orchestra performed in Frankfurt, Germany in 2012 — with 7,548 musicians.
Elvis Presley won three Grammy Awards — all for gospel recordings.
The first platinum album was the Eagles' 'Their Greatest Hits' in 1976.
The phrase 'the whole nine yards' may originate from the length of a WWII ammunition belt — though this is disputed.
'Wonderwall' by Oasis is the most played song on UK radio of the last 30 years.
Music reduces cortisol levels — the hormone associated with stress.
Hans Zimmer taught himself to play the piano — he has no formal music training.
The longest music video ever made is 'Meant for You' by Warrant, lasting 18 hours.
Leonard Bernstein was one of the first US-born conductors to achieve international fame.
The first concert ever livestreamed on the internet was by Severe Tire Damage in 1993.
Amy Winehouse had a contralto voice — one of the lowest female voice types.
John Williams has been nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Score 54 times — more than any other person.
Billie Eilish was 17 when her debut album 'When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?' went straight to number one in 2019.
The tuba was invented in 1835 — making it one of the newest members of the brass family.
'Running Up That Hill' by Kate Bush was released in 1985 and reached new heights of popularity after featuring in Stranger Things in 2022.
The world record for the longest recorded applause is 80 minutes for Plácido Domingo at the Vienna State Opera in 1991.

The world's most recorded composer is anonymous — the 'Greensleeves' melody has been recorded thousands of times.
Hip-hop became the most consumed music genre in the US in 2017, overtaking rock for the first time.
Paul Simon wrote 'The Sound of Silence' when he was just 21.
Louis Armstrong was buried with a trumpet mouthpiece.
The melody of 'We Wish You a Merry Christmas' dates back to 16th century England.
